title: Software Testing Fundamentals — CS coursework reading (likely OSU CS362)
html:
TL;DR

  • A long-form course reading on software testing fundamentals — feels like an Oregon State CS reading (likely CS 362: Software Engineering II based on title prefix "03 -" + the manual-vs-automated framing).
  • Defines testing's essence: "comparing actual behavior against expected behavior" — same equation in both manual and automated forms.
  • Characteristics of a good automated test: single specific behavior, specific input, observable results, known expected results, controlled environment.
  • "Tests should be unchanging" — once written, a test should not need to change unless the underlying behavior changes; pulling on a test during refactor/feature-add is a smell that the change is silently altering existing behavior.
  • Anti-pattern: testing implementation details — relying on internal variables (e.g., a showDialog boolean) makes tests brittle to refactors that rename or eliminate the internal.

Key quotes

"Testing shows the presence, not the absence of bugs." — Dijkstra, preserved in the reading as the load-bearing humility line.

"Inadequate software testing costs the US economy anywhere between tens of billions of dollars to more than two trillion dollars." — the economic-impact framing the reading uses to justify the discipline.

The reading also stacks failure-cost examples into a hierarchy: confusion → frustration → discomfort → expensive disaster (Ariane V88) → fatality (Therac-25). Same canonical CS-curriculum examples that appear in every intro testing module.

Why this is in the vault

Yahir's cs coursework page already aggregates other OSU readings; this is one of the more substantive ones from spring 2026. Notable that the reading lands one day after the "I'm drowning" entry — Yahir keeps cranking on coursework even while the Emily arc is fraying and Rankett feels like a treadmill. School is the steady surface.

What's worth pulling forward

  • The "don't test implementation details" idea is directly applicable to how the Rankett AI-visibility analysis pipeline should be tested — test the public score-output API, not the internal scoring weights.
  • "Tests should be unchanging" maps to the ghost white label commitment: external surface contracts should be stable even as internal fulfillment refactors.

Connections
